title: The Web Weaves Its Way into History
story: Imagine a world without websites. That changed in 1991 when Tim Berners-Lee unveiled his groundbreaking idea: the World Wide Web. On August 6, he released files explaining his vision and launched the WWW as a public service on the internet. This digital revolution connected people and information like never before, transforming how we communicate, learn, and explore the world from our screens.
activity_question: What did Tim Berners-Lee unveil in 1991?
follow_up_question: How do you think the World Wide Web has changed the way we live since 1991?

title: Boy Makes Waves in Channel Swim
story: Splash! A determined 12-year-old boy named Marcus Hooper dove into the chilly waters of the English Channel. On August 6, 1979, he set out to prove that big dreams come in small packages. His amazing feat showed that with courage and hard work, even kids can make a big splash in the world of sports!

story: A space rock shook the scientific world! On August 6, 1996, NASA made a jaw-dropping announcement about a meteorite found in Antarctica. title: Stargazers Spot a Cosmic Explosion
story: A dazzling light burst into the night sky on August 6, 1181. Astronomers in China and Japan marveled at a new star in the constellation Cassiopeia. title: Gertrude Makes Waves Across the Channel
story: Splash! Twenty-year-old Gertrude Ederle dove into the chilly English Channel on August 6, 1926. title: Cosmonaut Circles Earth 17 Times
story: Zoom! On August 6, 1961, Gherman Titov blasted off in Vostok 2, becoming the second Russian to reach space. Titov paved the way for future space explorers to stay in orbit for days, weeks, and even months.

Weighing more than an elephant, it took a special furnace to melt all the metal needed. Little did the bell-makers know their creation would become a beloved symbol of Britain!

bonus_fact: Big Ben actually refers to the bell, not the clock or tower!
